title: Hen Party Boat Estepona: Celebration Ideas
excerpt: Planning a hen party in Estepona? Renting a private boat with a skipper is the ultimate way to celebrate. We cover boat types, costs, itineraries, and local tips for an unforgettable day on the Costa del Sol.
metaDescription: Hen party boat Estepona: the ultimate celebration guide. Discover boat types, costs, itineraries, and local tips for an unforgettable day on the Costa del Sol.
author: Elena Ruiz
content:
The 30-second answer

If you are organising a hen party in Estepona, renting a private boat with a licensed skipper is the most hassle-free way to celebrate. You get your own floating party venue, a dedicated crew, and the freedom to swim, sunbathe, and dance along the Costa del Sol. Expect to pay from around EUR 1,200 for a half-day charter on a motor yacht for 10 people, with options ranging from catamarans to RIBs and even licence-free day boats. Departure ports include Marbella, Puerto Banús, and Estepona Marina, so you can pick the one that suits your group best. For more ideas, check out BoatHire24 for a full list of boats and prices.

Why Estepona for a hen party boat

Estepona is a solid choice for a hen party because it dodges the full-on chaos of Puerto Banús while still offering plenty of glamour. The old town has white-washed streets, flower-filled squares, and a relaxed vibe that works well for a pre-boat brunch or post-charter dinner. The marina itself is modern, with a handful of bars and restaurants right on the water, so you can step off the boat and keep the party going without a taxi ride.

The coastline here is a mix of sandy beaches and rocky coves, which means your skipper can drop anchor at spots like Cala del Faro or the quieter stretches near the Río Verde estuary. You get clear water for swimming, and the backdrop of the Sierra Bermeja mountains makes for decent photos. Compared to Marbella or Benalmádena, Estepona feels less crowded, especially in peak season, so your group won't be fighting for space on the water.

Another practical point: Estepona is roughly halfway between Gibraltar and Marbella, so if some of your guests are flying into Málaga or Gibraltar airports, the drive is manageable. The marina has parking nearby, and there are supermarkets and shops within walking distance for stocking up on drinks and snacks. For more local insights, read our blog on Costa del Sol marinas.

Boat types for hen parties

Not all boats are created equal a hen party. Here is a quick breakdown of what works best for different group sizes and budgets.

Boat typeBest forTypical capacityPrice range (half-day)
Motor yachtLarger groups, dancing, sunbathing10-20 peopleEUR 1,200-3,000
CatamaranStability, space, lounging12-30 peopleEUR 1,500-4,000
Sailing yachtSmaller groups, classic vibe6-12 peopleEUR 800-2,000
RIB / speedboatAdventure, watersports6-10 peopleEUR 600-1,200
Licence-free day boatBudget-friendly, self-drive6-8 peopleEUR 400-800

Motor yachts are the most popular hen party choice because they have a spacious flybridge or aft deck for dancing, a cabin for changing, and usually a small kitchen. Catamarans offer even more deck space and are more stable, so if anyone in your group gets seasick, this is the safer bet. RIBs are great if you want to add some speed or water toys, but they lack shade and toilet facilities. For a full list of options, browse our search page and filter by boat type and capacity.

What is included in a charter

Every charter on BoatHire24 comes with a licensed skipper who handles the navigation, safety, and anchoring. That means you can focus on the party without worrying about driving the boat or getting lost. The skipper usually speaks English and Spanish, and many are happy to act as a photographer or DJ if you ask nicely.

Most charters include fuel, insurance, and basic safety equipment. Some include a crew member who can serve drinks or help with food, but that is not always standard. You should check the listing details or ask the owner before booking. Extras like a barbecue, paddleboard, snorkel gear, or a sound system might cost extra, but many boats come with a Bluetooth speaker or basic sound setup.

Food and drink are generally not included, but you can bring your own. The skipper will usually have a cooler with ice, and you can pre-order catering from local companies that deliver to the marina. Alternatively, some charters offer a catering add-on with tapas, paella, or a barbecue. If you want a floating party, you can bring a speaker, decorations, and a cake. Just check with the skipper first about any restrictions on glass bottles or confetti that might be messy to clean up.

Sample itinerary for a hen party day

A typical half-day charter lasts 4 hours, which is enough time to sail, swim, eat, and dance without feeling rushed. Here is a sample itinerary for a morning departure from Estepona Marina.

  • 10:00 Meet at the marina. Board the boat, meet the skipper, and do a quick safety briefing. Pop the first bottle of cava.
  • 10:30 Depart. Head east towards the coast of Marbella, passing the beaches of El Cristo and the cliffs of Punta de la Doncella. The skipper will give you a running commentary or let you blast music.
  • 11:30 Drop anchor at a calm cove near Cala del Faro. Swim, snorkel, paddleboard, or just float on a lilo. The water is usually clear and around 22-26°C in summer.
  • 12:30 Lunch on board. Serve tapas, paella, or a picnic. The skipper can put up a sunshade or let you eat in the cockpit.
  • 13:30 Cruise back towards Estepona. If time allows, stop for a quick photo by the marina entrance or spot some dolphins if you are lucky.
  • 14:00 Return to port. Disembark and head to a nearby restaurant for a post-charter meal or continue the party at a beach bar.

For a full-day charter (8 hours), you can extend the route to include Puerto Banús for a lap of the superyachts, or head west towards the Strait of Gibraltar where dolphin sightings are almost guaranteed. You can also add a stop at a beach club for lunch on land, but that usually means an extra fee for mooring.

Costs and budgeting tips

Hen party boat charters in Estepona vary widely depending on the season, boat size, and duration. Here are some rough figures to help you budget.

  • Half-day (4 hours): from EUR 600 for a RIB up to EUR 3,000 for a luxury motor yacht.
  • Full-day (8 hours): from EUR 1,000 to EUR 5,000+.
  • Deposit: typically 30-50% of the total, refundable if you cancel within the agreed window.
  • Extras: catering EUR 20-40 per person, water toys EUR 50-100, sound system upgrade EUR 100-200.

To keep costs down, choose a weekday charter (Monday-Thursday) when prices are lower. Split the cost among the group: for 10 people, a EUR 1,500 charter works out at EUR 150 per person, which is cheaper than a night out in a club. You can also bring your own food and drink to avoid catering fees. Just remember to bring plenty of ice and disposable cups to avoid breakages.

Another tip: book early. Estepona is less busy than Marbella, but popular boats still get snapped up in July and August. Aim to book at least 4-6 weeks ahead for summer dates. For more advice on budgeting, see our guide to boat rental costs on the Costa del Sol.

What to bring and what to leave behind

Packing for a hen party boat is straightforward, but there are a few things that make the day smoother. Here is a checklist.

  • Essentials: swimwear, towel, sunscreen (reef-safe if possible), sunglasses, hat, a light jacket or cover-up for the breeze.
  • Party gear: a Bluetooth speaker (though many boats have one), decorations (avoid confetti or glitter that is hard to clean), a banner or bunting, and a cake or cupcakes.
  • Food and drink: plenty of water, soft drinks, wine, beer, or cava. Ice is usually provided, but check. Snacks like crisps, fruit, and sandwiches are easy to eat on board.
  • Extras: seasickness tablets (if anyone is prone), a waterproof phone case, a camera, and a change of clothes for after the charter.

Leave behind anything that could break or cause a mess: glass bottles (most skippers prefer cans or plastic), high heels (flat shoes or barefoot is better), and any illegal substances. The skipper can refuse to sail if they see drugs on board, so keep it legal. Also, avoid bringing a massive cake that needs refrigeration unless the boat has a fridge big enough. Check with the owner beforehand if you have questions.

Best time of year for a hen party boat

The Costa del Sol has a Mediterranean climate with hot summers and mild winters. The best time for a hen party boat is from May to October, when the sea temperature is above 20°C and the chance of rain is low. July and August are the hottest months, with temperatures reaching 30-35°C, so you will want plenty of shade and sunscreen. September and October are also good, with slightly cooler weather and fewer crowds.

If you are planning a winter hen party (November to April), you can still charter a boat, but you will need to dress warmly and accept that swimming might be a quick dip rather than a long session. The sea temperature drops to around 15-18°C, and the wind can pick up. Some boats have heated cabins, so check with the owner. Winter rates are lower, and you might have the coastline almost to yourself.

For sunset charters, aim for a departure around 17:00 in summer or 15:00 in winter. The light is beautiful, and you can watch the sun sink behind the Strait of Gibraltar. Just remember that sunset is earlier in winter, so plan accordingly.

Common questions

Do we need a licence to drive the boat? No. Every charter includes a licensed skipper who handles all navigation. You can relax and enjoy the ride.

Can we bring our own alcohol? Yes, most charters allow you to bring your own drinks. Some might charge a corkage fee if you want them to serve it, but generally it is fine.

What happens if the weather is bad? The skipper will decide if it is safe to sail. If the charter is cancelled due to weather, you will usually get a full refund or the option to reschedule. Check the cancellation policy before booking.

Is there a toilet on board? Most motor yachts and catamarans have a toilet. RIBs and small day boats may not, so ask the owner if that is important for your group.

Can we play our own music? Yes. Most boats have a Bluetooth speaker or auxiliary input. Bring your own speaker if you want a backup.

How many people can we bring? It depends on the boat. Capacity ranges from 6 to 30+ people. Check the listing for the maximum number of guests allowed.

Do we need to tip the skipper? Tipping is not mandatory but is appreciated. A typical tip is 10-20% of the charter fee, given if you are happy with the service.

Choosing the right departure port

Estepona Marina is the obvious starting point, but you have other options within a 20-minute drive. Puerto Banús is the flashier choice, with superyachts and champagne bars right at the dock. If your hen party wants a glamorous send-off, boarding in Banús adds a layer of luxury. The downside is parking costs around EUR 30 per day and the marina is always busy. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is a middle ground, with decent restaurants and easier parking. Cabopino is quieter, with a sandy beach next to the marina, ideal if you want a pre-boat swim without crowds. For a full list of departure points, check our search page to filter by port.

Dolphin spotting and wildlife on the route

One of the best parts of a hen party boat from Estepona is the chance to see dolphins. The Strait of Gibraltar is a migration corridor, and bottlenose dolphins are common, especially if you head west towards the strait. In summer, you might also see pilot whales or even a fin whale if you are lucky. The skipper usually knows the best spots and will slow down for photo opportunities. For a guaranteed dolphin experience, choose a full-day charter that goes further west. Just be aware that wildlife sightings are never guaranteed, and the skipper will prioritise safety and comfort over chasing animals. If your group is keen on marine life, mention it when booking so the skipper can plan the route accordingly.

Adding watersports and activities

If your hen party is active, many boats offer extras like paddleboards, kayaks, or snorkel gear. A paddleboard is great for Instagram photos, and snorkelling in the clear waters near Cala del Faro can reveal sea bream and octopus. Some RIBs and motor yachts can tow a banana boat or a ringo, which adds a fun, adrenaline-filled element. Expect to pay around EUR 50-100 for a paddleboard or snorkel set for the day. For a more relaxed activity, bring a floating mat or a lilo for lounging. Just check with the owner that the boat has space to store bulky equipment. If you want a watersports-focused day, a RIB or a motor yacht with a bathing platform is the best choice.

Evening options after the charter

Once you dock, Estepona has a good selection of restaurants and bars for continuing the celebration. For a proper hen party dinner, book a table at a restaurant in the old town, like those on Plaza de las Flores or Calle Terraza. Many places offer group menus for EUR 25-40 per person, including paella, grilled fish, and sangria. For a livelier vibe, head to the marina bars like La Rada or the beach clubs along the promenade. If you want to keep the party going, Puerto Banús is a 15-minute taxi ride away, with clubs like Aqwa Mist and Pangea. Taxis from Estepona to Banús cost around EUR 20-25. For a more local experience, try the chiringuitos (beach bars) on the Playa de la Rada, which often have live music in summer.

Seasonal pricing and booking windows

Prices for hen party boats in Estepona follow a clear seasonal pattern. June and September offer the best balance of good weather and reasonable rates, with half-day charters often 20-30% cheaper than July and August. July and August are peak season, and you will pay a premium for popular boats. If you are flexible, consider a late May or early October charter, when the sea is still warm and prices drop further. Booking early is essential for summer: popular boats for groups of 10-15 people can be fully booked by May. For last-minute bookings, you might find deals on weekdays or smaller boats like RIBs. Always confirm the total cost including any extras before paying the deposit, as some owners charge extra for cleaning or fuel.

title: Marbella Boat Photoshoot Tips: Best Poses
excerpt: Practical, cynical advice on pulling off a boat photoshoot in Marbella: best poses, light, angles, and how to avoid looking like a rental brochure reject.
metaDescription: Marbella boat photoshoot tips: best poses, light, angles, and local spots for a shoot that doesn't scream 'rental brochure'. Skip the clichés.
author: James Whitfield
content:
The 30-second answer

You want boat photos that don't look like a rental brochure reject. Marbella boat photoshoot tips: shoot at golden hour (6-8 PM in summer), use the bow for hero shots, keep the skipper out of frame, and avoid the 'I'm freezing in a bikini' face. Puerto Banús gives you luxury backdrops; Cabopino gives you empty sand. Pick your vibe, but remember: the boat is a prop, not the star.

Why a boat photoshoot in Marbella is not a holiday snap

You've seen the Instagram grid. A woman in a white dress on a yacht bow, sunset behind her, La Concha mountain in the distance. It looks effortless. It is not. The reality is wind, salt spray, and a skipper who wants to get back to port before the bar closes. A boat photoshoot in Marbella requires planning, because the Mediterranean sun is brutal and the sea breeze will turn your hair into a horror movie wig.

Marbella's coastline runs from Cabopino in the east to Sotogrande in the west, with Puerto Banús as the glittery centre. Each port has a different flavour. Puerto Banús is for the superyacht set: think polished wood, white leather, and a backdrop of designer shops. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is more relaxed, with fishing boats and smaller motor yachts. Estepona offers a quieter, more traditional Andalusian vibe. For a shoot, you want a boat that matches your aesthetic. A RIB or speedboat works for edgy, fast-paced shots; a catamaran gives you deck space and a trampoline for lying down. Sailing yachts offer classic lines and wooden details. Superyachts are price on request, but they deliver the ultimate luxury look.

Before you book, check the boat's condition. A chipped gel coat or faded upholstery will not edit out easily. Ask the charter company for recent photos. And for the love of god, do not wear heels on a teak deck. You will slip, you will fall, and the skipper will laugh.

Best light for Marbella boat photoshoots: golden hour or bust

Marbella sits at 36 degrees north, so the sun is high and harsh from 11 AM to 4 PM in summer. That is not your friend. The best light for a boat photoshoot is the golden hour, roughly 6-8 PM in June through August, or 5-7 PM in September. The sun is low, the light is warm, and the sea turns a deep blue-green. You get soft shadows and a glow that makes your skin look like you drink collagen for breakfast.

If you must shoot midday, find shade. The bimini top on a motor yacht is your best bet. Or shoot from inside the cabin, using the windows as natural frames. Avoid direct overhead light; it creates harsh shadows under your eyes and nose. That is not a good look. Also, the temperature at midday in July hits 32-35 degrees C. You will sweat. Your makeup will melt. Your hair will frizz. Do not do it.

Cloudy days are underrated. An overcast sky acts as a giant softbox, diffusing the light evenly. You get no harsh shadows, and the sea looks moody. It works for a more editorial, less holiday-snap aesthetic. But check the wind forecast. If the levante wind is blowing (east wind, common in summer), the sea gets choppy and the boat rocks. That makes for blurry shots and seasick models.

Best poses for a boat photoshoot: the ones that don't look like you're trying too hard

You are on a boat, not a fashion runway. The best poses for a boat photoshoot are natural, relaxed, and use the boat's structure. The bow is the classic spot: sit on the edge, legs dangling, looking out to sea. Or stand at the bow, one hand on the railing, wind in your hair. Do not look at the camera. Look at the horizon. It creates a sense of aspiration, like you are about to sail into a better life.

The stern is good for group shots. Sit on the sun loungers, facing each other, holding a glass of something cold. Avoid the 'cheers' cliché. Instead, have one person looking at the other, laughing. The flybridge (upper deck) gives you height and a wider view of the coast. Lean against the helm, or sit on the edge of the sofa. Keep your hands busy: hold a hat, a towel, a book. Do not hold your phone. That ruins the illusion.

For a catamaran, use the trampoline net at the front. Lie on your stomach, head propped up on your hands, feet in the air. Or sit cross-legged, facing the camera, with the sea behind you. For a sailing yacht, the boom and mast make good frames. Stand under the boom, looking up at the sails. Or sit on the cabin roof, legs crossed, with the wheel in the background. The key is to interact with the boat, not just stand on it like a mannequin.

Avoid the 'skipper' pose: you at the wheel, looking serious. That is for the charter brochure. You are not the captain. You are the guest. Act like it.

What to wear for a boat photoshoot in Marbella: fabric, colour, and common sense

Fabric matters. Cotton and linen crease in the sea breeze. Synthetics look cheap. Go for natural fibres with some weight: silk, viscose, or a structured cotton. Colour: white, cream, navy, or pastels. Avoid black; it absorbs heat and looks funereal. Red works if you want a pop, but it fights with the sunset. Neutrals blend with the boat and the sea, making you the focal point.

Footwear: flat sandals or barefoot. No heels. Heels on a teak deck leave marks, and you will slip. The skipper will not be impressed. If you must wear heels for a standing shot, bring them in a bag and put them on for two minutes. Then take them off.

Sunscreen: wear it. SPF 50, waterproof, matte finish. Shiny skin is not editorial; it is sweaty. Reapply every two hours. And bring a hat. Not a baseball cap. A wide-brimmed straw hat. It protects your face and gives you a prop. Sunglasses: aviators or oversized frames. No sports sunglasses. You are not fishing.

Hair: if the wind is up, tie it back. A low bun or a braid works. Loose hair will become a tangled mess. If you want flowing hair shots, do them at anchor in a sheltered cove, not at cruising speed. The Cala del Faro, near Estepona, is a good spot. Calm water, cliffs, and a lighthouse in the background. Worth the extra fuel cost.

Locations for a boat photoshoot in Marbella: from Puerto Banús to Cabopino

Puerto Banús is the obvious choice. The marina is full of superyachts, the backdrop is luxury shops and palm trees, and the light at sunset is spectacular. But it is busy. You will have other boats in the background. To avoid that, shoot from the bow looking out to sea, or use a tight frame. The Rio Verde river mouth, just east of the port, gives you a view of La Concha mountain behind you. That is the classic Marbella shot.

Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is quieter. Smaller boats, fewer crowds. Good for a more intimate shoot. The fishing port at the west end has colourful boats and a working harbour feel. Cabopino, east of Marbella, has a small marina and a long sandy beach. The dunes behind the beach are a protected area, so no buildings in the background. Perfect for a natural, less glitzy look.

Estepona old town is visible from the sea, with its white houses and flower pots. Anchor near the marina and use the town as a backdrop. Sotogrande is exclusive, with large villas and manicured gardens. The marina there is more private, but you need permission to shoot. Benalmádena, east of Marbella, has a large marina and a cable car. The mountains behind it give a dramatic backdrop.

For a dolphin shot, head west towards the Strait of Gibraltar. The waters off Tarifa are known for dolphin pods. But that is a two-hour cruise from Puerto Banús. You need a fast boat and a lot of fuel. And the dolphins do not pose. It is luck. But if you get it, the photo is worth it. Just do not expect it.

Camera settings and gear for boat photoshoots: keep it simple

You do not need a Hasselblad. A decent mirrorless camera or even a recent iPhone will do. The key is to control the light. Shoot in aperture priority mode, f/2.8 to f/4 for portraits (blurs the background), f/8 for group shots. ISO as low as possible, 100-400. Shutter speed at least 1/250 to freeze motion from the boat rocking. If the boat is moving, use 1/500 or higher.

A polarising filter is your friend. It cuts glare from the water and makes the sea look deeper blue. It also reduces reflections on glass and polished surfaces. A lens hood prevents flare from the sun. And bring a lens cloth. Salt spray will fog your lens in minutes. Wipe it between shots.

For video, use a gimbal. The boat rocks, the sea moves, and handheld footage looks like a disaster movie. A gimbal smooths it out. Shoot in 4K at 30fps, then crop for 1080p. That gives you room to stabilise in post. Do not use digital zoom. It destroys quality.

Backup your photos on the boat. Memory cards get wet, phones fall overboard. Use a waterproof case or a dry bag. And bring a power bank. The boat's USB ports are often slow or broken.

How to work with a skipper during a photoshoot: don't be a pain

Your skipper is not a model. They are there to drive the boat and keep you safe. Do not ask them to pose. Do not ask them to take photos. They are professionals, but they are not photographers. Respect their time. The charter is usually for a half-day (4 hours) or a full day (8 hours). A photoshoot should take 1-2 hours max. The rest of the time, enjoy the boat. Drink something. Swim. The skipper will appreciate it.

Tell the skipper your plan before you leave port. 'We want to anchor at Cala del Faro for 30 minutes, then cruise slowly past the coast for some shots.' They will know the best spots and the wind conditions. Do not change the plan mid-cruise without asking. And do not expect them to wait while you change outfits. Bring a small bag with your clothes and change in the cabin. The skipper does not want to see you half-dressed.

Tipping: 10-15% of the charter fee is standard. Cash. Hand it to the skipper at the end. They will remember you and give you a better spot next time.

Common mistakes in Marbella boat photoshoots and how to avoid them

Mistake one: shooting at midday. You look sweaty, the light is harsh, and the sea is washed out. Mistake two: wearing white against a white boat. You disappear. Wear a colour that contrasts with the boat. Mistake three: posing like you are on a catwalk. Boats are not runways. You look stiff. Relax, move, laugh.

Mistake four: ignoring the wind. Check the forecast. If the wind is over 15 knots, the sea will be choppy and your hair will be everywhere. Reschedule. Mistake five: forgetting the sunscreen. You will burn in 20 minutes on the water. The reflection from the sea increases UV exposure. Mistake six: bringing too much gear. A camera, one lens, a polariser, a dry bag. That is it. You do not need a tripod on a moving boat. You do not need a drone unless you have permission from the port authority. Drones are banned in most marinas in Marbella. Check before you fly.

Mistake seven: not having a backup plan. What if it rains? What if the boat breaks down? What if the skipper cancels? Have a second date in mind. Marbella weather is usually good, but the levante wind can ruin a shoot. Be flexible.

Common questions

What is the best time of day for a boat photoshoot in Marbella?
Golden hour, 6-8 PM in summer, 5-7 PM in autumn. The light is warm and soft. Avoid midday.

What should I wear for a boat photoshoot in Marbella?
Neutral colours like white, cream, or navy. Flat sandals or barefoot. No heels. Bring a hat and sunglasses.

Can I bring a photographer on the charter?
Yes, but check with the charter company first. Some charge extra for additional guests. Most are fine with it.

How long does a boat photoshoot take?
1-2 hours for the shoot itself. Book a half-day charter (4 hours) to allow time for cruising and changing.

What are the best locations for a boat photoshoot in Marbella?
Puerto Banús for luxury, Cabopino for natural beaches, Cala del Faro for cliffs, and Estepona for old town views.

Do I need a permit for a drone on a boat photoshoot?
Yes, drones are restricted in most Marbella marinas. You need permission from the port authority and the skipper. Better to skip it.

Can I shoot on a superyacht?
Yes, but price on request. Superyachts are available for charter in Puerto Banús. Contact BoatHire24 for options.

title: Boat Party with DJ in Marbella: Best Music on the Water
excerpt: The best boat parties with DJ in Marbella, from Puerto Banús to Estepona. Motor yachts, catamarans, and RIBs with sound systems, skippers, and sunset vibes.
metaDescription: Plan a boat party with DJ in Marbella. Motor yachts, catamarans, and RIBs with sound systems. Depart Puerto Banús, Estepona, or Marbella Marina. Book now.
author: Carlos Mendoza
content:
The 30-second answer

You want a boat party with a DJ in Marbella, and the good news is it's straightforward to arrange. Most charters include a licensed skipper and a basic sound system, but for a proper DJ set you'll need a boat with a quality audio setup and space for dancing. Motor yachts from 40ft upwards and catamarans work best, departing from Puerto Banús, Marbella Marina, or Estepona. Expect to pay from around EUR 2,500 for a half-day charter with a DJ, including crew and fuel. You can browse options on BoatHire24 and filter by boat type and capacity.

Why a boat party with DJ beats a club

You've done the club thing in Puerto Banús. You've queued for drinks, fought for space on the dance floor, and ended up smelling of other people's sweat. A boat party with a DJ in Marbella is the opposite. You're on the Mediterranean, with La Concha mountain behind you and the coast of Africa visible on a clear day. The music hits different when the bass travels across open water.

You get your own private space, your own playlist (or a professional DJ who reads the crowd), and the freedom to anchor in a quiet cove for a swim. The boat becomes a floating nightclub, but one where you can jump off and cool down between tracks. Most charters run from late afternoon to sunset, which is when the light turns golden and the sea breeze picks up.

The social dynamic is better too. No strangers pushing past you. No bouncers. Just your group, a DJ who knows what works, and the open water. If you're celebrating a birthday, a bachelor or bachelorette party, or just want an excuse to gather friends, this is the format that delivers.

You'll find options on our search page that let you filter by boat type, capacity, and price range. Most operators in Marbella are flexible and can arrange a DJ on request even if it's not listed upfront.

Best boats for a DJ party

Not every boat works for a party with a DJ. You need deck space, a decent sound system, and enough power to run the gear without draining the batteries. Here are the three types that suit the job.

Motor yachts (40-60ft): These are the most common choice. They have a large aft deck and a flybridge, both of which can host a DJ setup. The sound carries well, and the yacht's generator handles the power. Examples include the Azimut 50 or the Sunseeker 46. Capacity is usually 10-12 guests for a party charter. Prices from around EUR 3,000 for a half day.

Catamarans (40-50ft): Catamarans offer massive deck space because the hulls are wide and the cockpit is open. They're stable, which means less seasickness and better dancing. A Lagoon 450 or similar can take 12-15 guests. The sound system needs to be positioned carefully to avoid feedback, but the open layout works well. Prices from around EUR 2,800 for a half day.

RIB or speedboat (30-40ft): These are for smaller groups who want a loud, fast party. They have a single deck and limited seating, but the open design means the music projects directly. Good for 6-8 guests. Prices from around EUR 1,500 for a half day.

You can browse all options on BoatHire24 and contact the operator directly to confirm DJ availability and sound system specs.

What to expect from the DJ and sound system

When you book a boat party with a DJ in Marbella, the DJ is usually an add-on rather than included in the base price. The operator will either have a DJ they work with regularly or can recommend one. Expect to pay an extra EUR 300-600 for a professional DJ for a half-day charter, depending on their reputation and equipment.

The sound system on the boat itself matters. Many yachts have a built-in Fusion or Bose marine audio system with Bluetooth, but for a proper party you want something with more grunt. A good operator will provide a portable PA system or a DJ controller that connects to the boat's speakers. Ask about this when you enquire.

You should also discuss the music style. Most DJs can adapt, but it helps to give them a few reference tracks. House, tech house, and commercial pop work well for daytime parties. If you want something specific, like reggaeton or hip-hop, say so in advance.

The DJ will typically set up on the aft deck or the flybridge, depending on where the power outlets are. They'll bring their own laptop or USB drive, mixer, and sometimes a small speaker array. The boat's generator or inverter handles the power, but check that it can support the load if the DJ brings serious equipment.

For a list of boats that can accommodate a DJ setup, check the search page and filter by 'entertainment system' or 'sound system'.

Departure ports and routes

Your boat party with DJ in Marbella can depart from several ports along the Costa del Sol. The most popular is Puerto Banús, the glitzy marina full of superyachts and designer shops. It's the natural starting point for a party crowd. From here, you can cruise towards the Cala del Faro or the Río Verde area, where there are calm anchorages for swimming and dancing.

Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is a quieter alternative, closer to the old town. You get a more relaxed departure and are out on the water faster. Routes from here often head east towards Cabopino or west towards Puerto Banús.

Estepona is further west and less crowded. The old town is charming, and the coastline has several small coves. You can anchor near the Estepona lighthouse for a sunset party. The water is usually calmer on this stretch.

Benalmádena and Sotogrande are also options, though less common for party charters. Sotogrande is more exclusive and quieter, while Benalmádena has a lively marina with bars and restaurants for after the boat.

Typical routes last 3-4 hours and cover 10-20 nautical miles. You stop once or twice for swimming and anchor with the music playing. If you want to see dolphins, head towards the Strait of Gibraltar, though that adds travel time and costs more for fuel.

You can compare departure ports and boat options on BoatHire24.

What's included and what's extra

When you book a boat party with DJ in Marbella, the base charter usually includes the boat, the skipper, fuel for a standard route, and basic safety equipment. The DJ is an extra cost, as mentioned. Here's a breakdown of what to expect.

Included: Licensed skipper, fuel for up to 4 hours, dinghy and anchor, life jackets, sometimes a basic Bluetooth speaker. Some operators include a cooler with ice and bottled water.

Extra cost: DJ (EUR 300-600), food catering (EUR 20-40 per person for a buffet or platters), premium drinks package (EUR 15-30 per person), extra hours beyond the standard 4, and a larger sound system upgrade. Some boats charge extra for cleaning if you make a mess.

Not allowed: Red wine (stains the deck), glass bottles (safety hazard), professional photography without prior permission, and smoking inside the cabin. Most operators allow smoking on deck.

You should confirm the total cost before booking. Ask for a written quote that itemises the boat hire, DJ fee, food, and any extras. Most operators require a 50% deposit to secure the date, with the balance due on the day.

For a full list of boats with their inclusions, visit the search page and read the individual boat descriptions.

When to book and how many guests

The best time for a boat party with DJ in Marbella is from May to October, when the weather is warm and the sea is calm. July and August are peak season, with temperatures hitting 30 degrees C and water at 22-24 degrees C. Book at least two weeks in advance for weekends in August. June and September offer similar conditions with fewer crowds.

Group size matters. Most motor yachts and catamarans have a maximum capacity of 10-15 guests for party charters, because the skipper and DJ count towards the total. RIBs take 6-8. If you have a larger group, you can charter two boats and coordinate the music via radio, or look for a superyacht (60ft+) that can take 20+ guests. Superyacht charters start from around EUR 6,000 for a half day with a DJ.

For smaller groups of 4-6, a RIB or speedboat works well. You get a more intimate party and can move faster between spots. For groups of 8-12, a motor yacht or catamaran is ideal. For 15+, you need a larger vessel or multiple boats.

You can check availability and guest limits on BoatHire24 by entering your preferred date and group size.

Practical tips for a smooth party

You've booked your boat party with DJ in Marbella. Here's how to make sure it runs without drama.

Arrive early: Get to the marina 20 minutes before departure. The skipper will do a safety briefing and show you where the life jackets are. This also gives the DJ time to set up and test the sound.

Bring a playlist backup: Even with a professional DJ, have a USB drive or Spotify playlist ready in case the DJ's gear fails. Most boats have Bluetooth or an AUX input.

Sun protection: The sun reflects off the water and you will burn. Bring SPF 50, a hat, and sunglasses. The skipper usually has shade on the flybridge or under the bimini, but the dance floor is in the open.

Seasickness: If anyone in your group is prone to seasickness, take a preventative an hour before departure. The Mediterranean is usually calm in summer, but the motion is different from a club floor. Catamarans are more stable than monohulls.

Cash for tips: It's customary to tip the skipper and the DJ 10-15% of the total charter cost if the service is good. Cash is easiest.

No glass: Bring plastic cups or use the ones the operator provides. Glass breaks and is dangerous on deck.

For more tips on planning a boat party, check the BoatHire24 blog.

Combining with other activities

A boat party with DJ in Marbella doesn't have to be just music and swimming. You can add other elements to make the day more memorable.

Water toys: Many charters include a SUP board, a sea scooter, or a small inflatable. You can also rent a jet ski to follow the boat. Jet ski hire is separate and costs from around EUR 100 per hour.

Dining: Instead of a buffet on board, you can anchor near a beach club like the Ocean Club in Puerto Banús or the Chiringuito de la Playa in Estepona. The skipper will drop you off by dinghy. You eat ashore and return to the boat for more music.

Sunset cruise: The best light is from 7pm to 9pm in summer. You can start the party at 4pm, anchor for a swim, then cruise towards the sunset with the DJ playing a chill set that builds into the evening. Many operators offer a sunset add-on for an extra hour.

After-party: Some boats can dock at a marina and let you continue the party at a nearby club. Puerto Banús has several options within walking distance of the marina. The skipper will help with docking.

You can find boats that offer these extras on our search page by filtering for 'water toys' or 'catering'.

Common questions

Can I bring my own DJ? Yes, most operators allow it, but you need to confirm in advance. The DJ must have their own equipment and be insured. Some operators charge a fee for an external DJ.

What if it rains? The Costa del Sol has over 300 sunny days a year, but if the forecast is bad, the operator will usually offer a reschedule or a full refund. Check the cancellation policy when you book.

Is the skipper included? Yes, every charter on BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper. You don't need a boating licence.

Can we swim from the boat? Yes, the skipper will anchor in a safe spot. You can swim, snorkel, or use the water toys. The DJ keeps playing during the swim stop.

How loud can the music be? There are noise restrictions in some anchorages, especially near residential areas. The skipper will advise. In open water, you can play as loud as the system allows.

What's the alcohol policy? You can bring your own drinks, but glass bottles are banned. Most operators offer a drinks package if you prefer. The skipper will not serve anyone who is dangerously drunk.

How do I book? Browse boats on BoatHire24, select your date and group size, and send an enquiry. The operator will confirm availability and send a quote.

title: Best Time of Year for a Boat Trip in Marbella, Spain
excerpt: The best time for a boat trip in Marbella is May to October, offering warm weather, calm seas, and lively coastal scenes. Avoid August for crowds and July for heat; shoulder months like June and September are ideal.
metaDescription: Discover the best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella. From May to October, find ideal weather, calm seas, and tips for Puerto Banús and Costa del Sol char
author: James Whitfield
content:
The 30-second answer

The best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella is between May and October, when the Mediterranean is warm, the sun is reliable, and the sea is calm enough for a comfortable charter. For the sweet spot of good weather, fewer crowds, and reasonable prices, aim for June or September. July and August are hot and packed, but the water is at its warmest. Winter trips are possible but chilly and unpredictable.

Why the Costa del Sol has a long boating season

Marbella sits on the Costa del Sol, which translates to “Coast of the Sun” for a reason. The region gets over 300 days of sunshine a year, and the Mediterranean is protected from the Atlantic swell. That means you can technically charter a boat in February if you’re brave enough, but the real season runs from spring to autumn. The sea temperature in May is around 18 degrees C, climbing to 24-26 degrees C by August. Wind conditions are generally mild, with the Poniente breeze from the west keeping things pleasant. The Levante wind from the east can kick up chop, but it’s rare in summer. So the window for a comfortable, swim-friendly trip is wide, but not infinite.

Month-by-month breakdown

MonthSea temp (C)Air temp (C)CrowdsBest for
May18-2022-26LowQuiet escapes, sailing
June20-2326-30ModerateAll-round ideal
July23-2528-34HighSunbathing, partying
August24-2628-36Very highSwimming, but book ahead
September22-2426-30ModeratePerfect balance
October19-2222-26LowAutumn sun, dolphin watching

November to April sees cooler air (15-20 C) and sea temperatures dropping to 14-17 C. You can still go out on a sunny day, especially on a motor yacht with a cabin, but swimming is a challenge unless you’re hardy. The best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella if you want to swim is definitely June through September.

Peak season: July and August

July and August are the busiest months on the Costa del Sol. Puerto Banús is packed with superyachts and tourists, and the beaches are shoulder-to-shoulder. The upside: the sea is bath-warm, the sunsets are long, and the atmosphere is electric. You’ll find plenty of catamaran charters offering sunset trips with DJs and paella. But expect to pay premium prices. A half-day charter on a motor yacht might cost from around EUR 1,500 in August, compared to EUR 1,000 in June. Book well in advance. The water is perfect for swimming at Cala del Faro or off the coast of Estepona. If you don’t mind the crowds, this is a solid choice.

Shoulder season: May, June, September, October

These months are the hidden gems. May is spring-like, with wildflowers on the hills behind Marbella and the snow still visible on La Concha mountain. The sea is a bit cool for prolonged swimming, but fine for a splash. June is where it comes alive: warm, sunny, and not yet overrun. You can anchor off Cabopino and have a beach almost to yourself. September is the real winner. The crowds have thinned, the sea is still 24 C, and the light is golden. October can be hit or miss, but you often get a week of “veranillo” (little summer) with calm seas perfect for a trip to see dolphins in the Strait of Gibraltar. For the best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella that balances comfort and cost, June and September are unbeatable.

Winter charters: possible but different

From November to March, most commercial charters slow down, but you can still find options, especially for sailing yachts or RIBs. The air temperature hovers around 15-20 C, and the sea is choppier. You’ll need a windbreaker and maybe a jumper. The appeal is solitude: you can cruise past Puerto Banús without the summer chaos, and the views of the Sierra Blanca mountains are clearer. Some operators offer winter whale-watching trips to see migrating orcas and fin whales in the Strait. It’s not the classic Marbella experience, but it has its charm. Just don’t expect to swim.

What about Easter and spring breaks?

Easter (March or April) is a popular time for tourists, but the weather is a gamble. You might get 25 C and sun, or 15 C and rain. The sea is cold (around 16 C). If you’re set on a spring trip, focus on a motor yacht with a covered deck or a sailing yacht where you can stay dry. The advantage: the water is clear, and you might spot dolphins off Sotogrande. It’s not the best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella if you want to sunbathe, but it’s fine for a scenic cruise. Book a fishing boat charter and try for sea bass or bream, which are active in spring.

Local events and their impact on your trip

Marbella’s calendar affects boat availability and atmosphere. The Marbella International Film Festival in June brings a glam crowd. The Feria de San Bernabé in June fills the town with music and fairgrounds. The Puerto Banús Boat Show in April or May showcases new yachts. August’s Feria de Estepona is a week-long party. If you want a quiet trip, avoid these dates. If you want to see the Costa del Sol at its most vibrant, embrace them. Either way, book your BoatHire24 charter early for any event period. The best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella might coincide with a local fiesta, so check the calendar before you book.

Tips for choosing your ideal month

  • Swimming: July to September (sea 23-26 C).
  • Sunbathing without sweating: May, June, October.
  • Dolphin watching: April-May and September-October.
  • Fishing: Spring and autumn for variety.
  • Budget: May, October, and November offer lower rates.
  • Partying: July and August, especially in Puerto Banús.
  • Calm seas: June and September have the least wind.

For a search of available boats, you can filter by month and see what’s on offer. The best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella ultimately depends on what you want, but June and September are the safest bets.

Common questions

What is the best month for a boat trip in Marbella?
June and September are the best months for good weather, warm seas, and fewer crowds. May and October are also good but cooler.

Can you go boating in Marbella in winter?
Yes, but it’s chilly and less reliable. You’ll need warm clothes, and swimming is not enjoyable. Some charters run for whale watching or scenic cruises.

Is August too hot for a boat trip in Marbella?
August is hot (up to 36 C), but the sea is warm and the breeze helps. It’s crowded and expensive, so book early. It’s fine if you like the buzz.

Do I need to book a boat in advance?
Yes, especially for July and August. For shoulder months, a week or two ahead is usually fine. Use BoatHire24 to compare options.

What should I wear on a boat trip in Marbella?
In summer, shorts, swimwear, sunglasses, and a hat. In spring or autumn, bring a light jacket or jumper. Non-slip shoes are recommended.

Are there any bad months for sea conditions?
November to February can have strong Levante winds and rough seas. March and April are variable. Check the forecast before booking.

Can I see dolphins year-round in Marbella?
Dolphins are present all year, but the best sightings are in spring and autumn when they follow fish. Summer is also good, but more boats mean more competition for views.

What is the cheapest time to charter a boat in Marbella?
November to March are the cheapest, but with limited availability and cool weather. May and October offer good value with better conditions.

For more tips, check our blog on Marbella boating.

Why Puerto Banús is the best departure point in high season

Puerto Banús is the epicentre of Marbella’s boating scene, and for good reason. In July and August, the marina is a parade of superyachts, Ferraris, and designer boutiques. Departing from here puts you minutes from the open sea, and the short cruise past the Golden Mile gives you a front-row view of the mansions. You can head west to the dunes of Cabopino in 20 minutes or east to Cala del Faro in 30. The downside: berthing fees are high, and the marina is chaotic in peak season. If you want a quieter start, try Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo), which is less flashy but cheaper and closer to the old town. For a luxury experience, Puerto Banús is unbeatable, but book your skipper well ahead.

How the wind affects your trip: Poniente vs Levante

The Costa del Sol has two main winds that dictate sea conditions. The Poniente blows from the west, bringing calm seas and clear skies, common in summer. The Levante comes from the east, often kicking up a chop of 1-2 metres and making the sea uncomfortable for small boats. Levante is most frequent in spring and autumn, so if you book a RIB or speedboat in April or October, check the forecast. A catamaran handles chop better than a motor yacht, but no boat is fun in a swell. June and September have the lowest Levante frequency, with only 5-10 days of strong wind. For a smooth ride, choose those months and depart early, before the afternoon breeze picks up.

What to expect at Cala del Faro in different seasons

Cala del Faro is a small cove east of Marbella, near the lighthouse. In May, it’s quiet, with few boats and clear water, but the sea is 18 C, so swimming is brief. By June, the water warms to 21 C, and the cove gets busier, but you can still anchor without jostling. July and August see a flotilla of boats, with music from nearby yachts and the smell of barbecues on the beach. The water is 24-26 C, perfect for long swims. In September, the crowds thin, and the light is softer, making for a peaceful afternoon. October may have cooler water (20 C), but the cove is almost empty. For a swim-focused trip, aim for July to September. For solitude, May or October work.

Dolphin watching in the Strait of Gibraltar: timing matters

The Strait of Gibraltar is a 40-60 minute cruise from Marbella, depending on your boat. Dolphin species vary by season: common dolphins and striped dolphins are present year-round, but pilot whales and orcas pass through in spring (March-May) and autumn (September-November). The best time of year for a boat trip in Marbella if you want to see orcas is September, when they follow the bluefin tuna migration. You’ll need a fast boat, like a RIB or motor yacht, to reach the Strait quickly. Charter prices for a half-day dolphin trip start from around EUR 600 for a small boat. Book with a skipper who knows the area, as the Strait has strong currents and busy shipping lanes.

Fishing trips: the best months for catches

Marbella’s waters offer good fishing year-round, but the species change with the seasons. In spring (March-May), sea bass and bream are active near the coast, and you can catch them from a fishing boat charter. Summer (June-August) brings bonito and dorado, but you’ll need to go further offshore, around 5-10 nautical miles. Autumn (September-November) is prime for bluefin tuna, with fish up to 200 kg possible, but that requires a serious sportfishing boat and a skilled crew. Winter (December-February) is slower, but you can still catch hake and whiting. Charters cost from around EUR 400 for a half-day inshore trip to EUR 1,200 for a full-day offshore tuna hunt. Book in advance for tuna trips in September.

How to save money on a boat charter in Marbella

Charter prices vary wildly by season. In July and August, a half-day on a 40-foot motor yacht costs from around EUR 1,500. In May, June, September, or October, the same boat is from around EUR 1,000. For a budget option, consider a licence-free day boat, which you can drive yourself (no licence needed) for from EUR 300 per day. These are popular for small groups and best in calm months like June or September. Another tip: book a mid-week charter (Tuesday-Thursday) rather than weekends, as prices are 10-20% lower. Avoid the Feria de San Bernabé week in June, when demand spikes. Use BoatHire24 to compare boats and find last-minute deals in shoulder season.

What to pack for a boat trip in different months

Your packing list changes with the season. For May and October, bring a light jacket or fleece, as the sea breeze can be cool even on sunny days. Sunscreen is essential year-round, as the sun is strong from March to October. In July and August, pack a hat, sunglasses, and a rash guard for swimming, as the UV index is extreme. For winter trips (November-March), wear layers: a thermal base, a fleece, and a waterproof jacket. Non-slip shoes are a must for any month, as decks get wet. If you’re on a sailing yacht, bring a windproof jacket, as the wind feels colder at sea. Most charters provide towels and snorkelling gear, but check with your skipper.

Why Estepona is a hidden gem for autumn boat trips

Estepona, 20 minutes west of Marbella, has a quieter marina and a charming old town with flower-filled streets. In September and October, the weather is still warm (24-28 C air, 20-22 C sea), but the crowds are thin. You can anchor off the coast and have a beach to yourself, or cruise to the Strait for dolphin watching. The marina at Estepona is cheaper than Puerto Banús, and charter boats are often available at lower rates. Try a catamaran for a stable platform, ideal for autumn when the sea can have a slight swell. Estepona’s local restaurants serve grilled fish and paella, and you can tie up for lunch. For a relaxed, value-for-money trip, Estepona in September is hard to beat.

How to handle sea sickness on a Marbella boat trip

Sea sickness affects about 30% of people, but you can minimise it. The calmest months are June and September, when the sea is flattest. Avoid July and August if you’re prone to sickness, as the afternoon heat can make the cabin stuffy. Choose a catamaran over a motor yacht, as catamarans roll less. Take motion sickness tablets an hour before departure, and avoid heavy meals beforehand. Stay on deck and focus on the horizon, not your phone. If you feel queasy, ask the skipper to slow down or head closer to shore, where the water is calmer. Most charter boats have sick bags onboard, but it’s better to be prepared.

title: Starlite Festival Boat Party Marbella: Pre-Concert Fun
excerpt: Skip the pre-Starlite traffic. A boat party from Puerto Banús gets you to the festival on the water, with drinks, sunset views, and a skipper who knows the back way in.
metaDescription: Pre-Starlite boat party Marbella: cruise from Puerto Banús to the festival with drinks, sunset views, and a skipper. Book your charter now.
author: James Whitfield
content:
The 30-second answer

A Starlite boat party Marbella is the only sensible way to arrive at the festival. You skip the gridlocked N-340, sip a drink on the Mediterranean, and pull up to a dock reserved for charters. A licensed skipper handles everything. You just show up at Puerto Banús, board a motor yacht or catamaran, and let the boat do the work. The sunset over La Concha mountain is a free bonus. From around EUR 500 for a group of six, it is cheaper than a taxi and a VIP table, and you get a pre-party that starts before you hit the red carpet.

Why a boat beats the car every time

Starlite Festival is a pain to drive to. The venue sits in the Cantera de Nagüeles quarry, a 10-minute drive from Marbella town centre on a good day. On a Starlite night, the road turns into a car park. You sit in traffic, hunt for parking, and queue for security. A boat charter from Puerto Banús or Marbella Marina bypasses all that. The skipper drops you at the festival's private jetty, a 2-minute walk from the main entrance. No traffic. No parking. No stress. You arrive relaxed, slightly tipsy if you want, and ready to enjoy the show. Plus, the boat ride back is a calm end to the night, not a sweaty Uber scramble.

Which boat works for a Starlite party

You have options. A motor yacht from around 10 metres gives you a sun deck, a small bar, and a cabin for changing. A catamaran offers more deck space and stability, good for groups of 10 to 20 who want to stand and chat. A sailing yacht is slower but quieter, better if you want a chilled sunset cruise before the loud music. RIBs and speedboats are faster but cramped for a party. For a Starlite boat party Marbella, a motor yacht or catamaran is the sweet spot. Expect to pay from around EUR 500 for a 3-hour charter on a 10-metre motor yacht, up to EUR 2,000 for a 15-metre catamaran with crew and a sound system. Prices vary by season and demand. Always ask about fuel, VAT, and the dock fee at the festival jetty.

The route: from Puerto Banús to the quarry

The trip from Puerto Banús to the Starlite dock is about 4 nautical miles. At a cruising speed of 8 to 10 knots, it takes 25 to 35 minutes. You leave the marina, pass the golden sands of Marbella beach, and see La Concha mountain turn pink as the sun drops. The skipper will point out the old town of Marbella on your starboard side, the castle of San Pedro Alcántara to the port. You enter the small cove below the quarry, tie up at a floating pontoon that the festival reserves for boats. The whole thing feels exclusive, like you are smuggling yourself into the VIP area by sea. On the way back, you can stop for a night swim if the water is warm enough, usually 22 to 26 degrees C from June to September.

What you get on board: drinks, snacks, and a sound system

Most charters include a cooler with ice, bottled water, and soft drinks. You bring your own alcohol if the charter is bareboat, or you pay for a catered package. Some skippers will stop at a supermarket on the way out of the marina if you forget. A basic sound system is standard on most boats, but check if you want Bluetooth or an aux cable. For a proper party, you can rent a portable speaker or ask the crew to hook up their system. Food is usually a platter of jamón, cheese, and olives, but you can order a full tapas spread for an extra EUR 20 to EUR 40 per person. The key is to keep it simple. You are not there for a gourmet dinner. You are there to drink, dance a bit, and arrive at the festival in the right mood.

When to book and how to avoid disappointment

Starlite runs from July to August, with some shows in June and September. The most popular acts sell out weeks in advance, and so do the boat charters. Book your boat at least two weeks before the concert you want. If you leave it to the last minute, you will pay a premium or find nothing available. Saturday nights are the busiest. A midweek show is cheaper and quieter. The weather is almost always good, but check the forecast for wind. The skipper will cancel if the sea state is above 3 on the Beaufort scale, which is rare in summer. If you book through BoatHire24, you get a free cancellation window of 48 hours for most boats. Read the terms. Do not assume you can cancel on the day.

Combining Starlite with a day on the water

Why limit yourself to a pre-show cruise. You can charter the same boat for the whole day. Start at 10 am, head east towards the Cala del Faro or the Nerja caves, anchor for a swim and lunch, then turn back to Marbella in the late afternoon. Change into your concert clothes on board, have a shower if the boat has a freshwater deck shower, and cruise to the Starlite dock at sunset. That is a full day of sun, sea, and music for about EUR 1,200 to EUR 2,500 for a group of 10. It beats sitting in a hotel room waiting for the concert to start. If you want to see dolphins, ask the skipper to take the route past the Strait of Gibraltar on the way back, though that adds an hour to the trip. The dolphins are common in summer, especially around the Cabopino and Estepona areas.

Local tips for a smooth experience

Wear flat shoes on the boat. Heels damage the deck and are dangerous on a moving vessel. Bring a jacket or a light sweater. The sea breeze cools down fast after sunset. The festival venue is open air and can be warm, but the boat ride is chilly. Do not bring glass bottles. Most skippers ban them for safety. Transfer your drinks into plastic or cans. The crew will give you a bag for rubbish. Tip the skipper and the deckhand if they do a good job. EUR 20 to EUR 50 per person is standard for a charter. They will appreciate it and might let you stay a bit longer at the dock. Finally, confirm the exact meeting point in Puerto Banús. The marina has multiple docks. The skipper will text you the berth number on the day.

Common questions

Q: How many people can join a Starlite boat party Marbella?
A: A standard motor yacht holds 8 to 12 passengers. Catamarans hold 12 to 20. RIBs hold 6 to 8. Check the boat's passenger licence. The crew counts separately. Do not exceed the limit, or the skipper will refuse to sail.

Q: Is the boat party included in the Starlite ticket price?
A: No. The boat charter is separate. You must buy your Starlite ticket separately. The boat only gets you to the festival dock. It does not include entry to the concert.

Q: What happens if the weather is bad?
A: The skipper decides. If the sea state is unsafe, the charter is cancelled. You get a full refund or a reschedule. Starlite rarely cancels concerts for weather, but the boat trip might be called off. Check the forecast before you book.

Q: Can I bring my own food and drink?
A: Yes, on most boats. Some charters include a basic catering package. If you bring your own, use plastic or cans. Glass is not allowed. The crew can provide a cooler and ice.

Q: How do I get back from the festival after the concert?
A: The boat takes you back to Puerto Banús or Marbella Marina. The skipper waits at the dock. Agree on the return time before you depart. The boat leaves when you are all on board. Do not be late.

Q: Is a skipper included?
A: Yes. Every charter on BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper. You do not need a boating licence. The skipper handles navigation, mooring, and safety. You just enjoy the ride.

Q: What should I wear?
A: Flat shoes, a swimsuit if you want to swim during the day, a cover-up, and a jacket for the evening. The boat deck can be slippery. No heels. Sunglasses and sunscreen are essential. The sun is strong even at 5 pm.

Q: Can I book a boat for a private group?
A: Yes. All the charters are private. You and your guests are the only passengers. The boat does not mix with strangers. You can customise the route, the music, and the catering.

Which departure port gives you the best start

Puerto Banús is the obvious choice for a Starlite boat party Marbella. It is 4 NM from the festival dock, and the marina has a supermarket, a fuel station, and a dozen bars where you can meet your group before boarding. But it is not your only option. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is 2 NM closer, which shaves 10 minutes off the trip. The marina is quieter, cheaper for mooring, and has a direct view of La Concha mountain. If you are staying in the old town of Marbella, this is the smarter departure point. Cabopino is 8 NM east, too far for a short pre-concert cruise unless you make a day of it. Estepona is 12 NM west, which works if you want a longer sunset cruise before the show, but you pay extra for fuel. Sotogrande is 25 NM west, impractical for a same-night trip. Benalmádena is 20 NM east, also too far. Stick to Puerto Banús or Marbella Marina for a Starlite boat party. They are the only ports that make logistical sense.

The cost breakdown: what you actually pay for

A 3-hour charter for a Starlite boat party Marbella is not a flat fee. You pay the base charter rate, which covers the boat, the skipper, and basic insurance. Then you add fuel, typically EUR 50 to EUR 150 depending on the engine size and distance. VAT at 21 percent is added to the total. Some charters include a dock fee at the Starlite jetty, usually EUR 30 to EUR 60. Others charge it separately. Catering adds EUR 20 to EUR 40 per person for a tapas platter. A sound system upgrade costs EUR 50 to EUR 100. If you want a photographer on board, add EUR 150 to EUR 300. The total for a group of six on a 10-metre motor yacht is around EUR 600 to EUR 900 all in. For a 15-metre catamaran with 12 guests, expect EUR 1,500 to EUR 2,500. Always ask for a full quote in writing before you confirm. No one likes a surprise bill at the end of the night.

How to match the boat to the performer

The vibe of the concert changes the boat you want. If you are seeing a DJ or a pop act, go for a catamaran with a loud sound system and a dance-friendly deck. The wide beam of a Lagoon or Fountaine Pajot cat gives you room to move. If you are seeing a flamenco or acoustic performer, a classic sailing yacht like a Sun Odyssey or a Bavaria feels more intimate. The gentle motion and wooden interiors set a different mood. For a rock or indie concert, a motor yacht with a flybridge works well. You get an upper deck for dancing and a lower deck for quieter conversation. Do not overthink it. The boat is a means of transport and a pre-party venue. But matching the boat style to the artist makes the whole experience feel curated, not random.

The best time of year for a Starlite boat party

Starlite runs from early July to late August, with a few shows in June and September. July is the peak month. The water temperature is 24 to 26 degrees C, the sunset is around 9:30 pm, and the air stays warm until midnight. August is similar but busier. The marina fills up, and the festival crowd is larger. June and September are quieter. The water is cooler, 20 to 22 degrees C, but still swimmable. The sunset is earlier, around 9 pm, which means you can do a sunset cruise and still have time for the concert. The wind is lighter in June and September, so the sea is calmer. If you want a relaxed Starlite boat party Marbella without the chaos of peak summer, book a midweek show in June or September. You pay less for the boat and the tickets, and you get more space on the water.

What happens if the concert is sold out

You can still do a Starlite boat party Marbella even if the concert is sold out. The festival dock is open to boat charters regardless of whether you have a ticket. You cruise to the cove, anchor 50 metres from the shore, and listen to the music from the water. The sound carries well across the bay, especially for amplified acts. You see the lights of the stage and the crowd. It is not the same as being inside the venue, but it is a unique experience. The boat becomes your private VIP box. You avoid the queues, the crowds, and the high drink prices. You can swim between songs. The skipper will keep the boat at a safe distance and turn off the engine to avoid disturbing the show. This option works best for groups who want the atmosphere without the ticket cost.

How to handle the return trip after the concert

The concert ends around 1 am. The boat is waiting at the dock. You board, the skipper casts off, and you cruise back to the marina in 25 to 35 minutes. The sea is calm at night, and the lights of Marbella and Puerto Banús reflect on the water. The crew usually offers a nightcap, a soft drink or a glass of cava. You can ask for a short detour to see the stars away from the shore lights. The skipper will point out the constellation of Cassiopeia or the Milky Way if the sky is clear. Do not expect to swim. The water is dark, and the crew will not allow it for safety reasons. The return trip is a quiet wind-down. You arrive at the marina, say goodbye to the crew, and walk to your hotel or taxi. The whole thing takes less than an hour from the final encore to your bed.

Common mistakes people make with Starlite boat parties

The biggest mistake is booking the wrong boat size. A group of four does not need a 15-metre catamaran. A 10-metre motor yacht is more intimate and cheaper. The second mistake is forgetting to confirm the return time. The skipper will not wait past the agreed time. If you are late, you pay for an extra hour or find your own way back. The third mistake is not checking the boat's sound system. Some older boats have a basic FM radio. Bring a Bluetooth speaker if you want control over the playlist. The fourth mistake is wearing heels. The deck gets slippery, and the crew will ask you to remove them. The fifth mistake is assuming the charter includes Starlite tickets. It does not. Buy your tickets separately and early. The best concerts sell out months in advance. Avoid these mistakes, and your Starlite boat party Marbella will run smoothly.

  • How Much Does a Yacht Charter in Marbella Cost?
